{
  "instance_id": "med_skills_agent_001",
  "source_papers": [
    {
      "reference": "SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery",
      "rank": 1,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Core framework for automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical skill library construction from agent experiences",
      "usage": "Key methodological reference for skill discovery and organization in agent systems",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.08234"
    },
    {
      "reference": "ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs",
      "rank": 2,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Established framework for LLM tool-use with large-scale API integration, neural API retriever, and evaluation methodology",
      "usage": "Reference for tool/skill retrieval and integration architecture",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.16789"
    },
    {
      "reference": "AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem",
      "rank": 3,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Operating system for discovering, composing, and running skill pipelines from 90K+ skills using skill trees and DAG orchestration",
      "usage": "Reference for skill organization, retrieval, and pipeline composition",
      "url": "https://github.com/ynulihao/AgentSkillOS"
    },
    {
      "reference": "SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance",
      "rank": 4,
      "type": "evaluation",
      "justification": "First benchmark for evaluating agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains, showing +51.9pp improvement in healthcare domain",
      "usage": "Reference for evaluation methodology and evidence that skills are especially beneficial in healthcare",
      "url": "https://www.skillsbench.ai/"
    }
  ],
  "task2": "Build a Medical Skills library and Agent framework for medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention tasks, targeting MICCAI 2026. The project aims to: (1) Collect and curate existing open-source medical AI models and tools (e.g., segmentation models like MedSAM, classification models, detection models, registration models, report generation models) and encapsulate them as standardized, callable Medical Skills; (2) Design new skill modules to fill gaps in the medical skill ecosystem; (3) Build a Medical Agent powered by a large language model that can automatically discover, select, and compose appropriate Medical Skills to solve complex, multi-step medical tasks (e.g., a clinical workflow involving image preprocessing, organ segmentation, lesion detection, measurement, and report generation). The framework should support hierarchical skill organization, intelligent skill retrieval based on task requirements, and dynamic skill composition via execution graphs. The key innovation lies in bridging the gap between general-purpose agent skill frameworks (like ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL) and the specialized requirements of the medical imaging domain, where skills require domain-specific validation, safety constraints, and multi-modal data handling. The project should align with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by demonstrating practical clinical utility.",
  "task1": ""
}

input.content:
{
  "instance_id": "med_skills_agent_001",
  "source_papers": [
    {
      "reference": "SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery",
      "rank": 1,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Core framework for automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical skill library construction from agent experiences via RL",
      "usage": "Key methodological reference for skill discovery, SKILLBANK organization, and recursive skill evolution",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.08234"
    },
    {
      "reference": "ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs",
      "rank": 2,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Established framework for LLM tool-use with large-scale API integration, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning",
      "usage": "Reference for tool/skill retrieval architecture and standardized tool API format",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.16789"
    },
    {
      "reference": "AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem",
      "rank": 3,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "OS for discovering, composing, and running skill pipelines from 90K+ skills using skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG orchestration",
      "usage": "Reference for skill organization, retrieval, and pipeline composition architecture",
      "url": "https://github.com/ynulihao/AgentSkillOS"
    },
    {
      "reference": "SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance",
      "rank": 4,
      "type": "evaluation",
      "justification": "First benchmark for evaluating agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains (86 tasks, 7300+ trials). Shows +16pp average, +51.9pp in healthcare",
      "usage": "Evidence that skills are especially beneficial in healthcare; evaluation methodology reference",
      "url": "https://www.skillsbench.ai/"
    },
    {
      "reference": "TxAgent: An AI Agent for Therapeutic Reasoning Across a Universe of Tools",
      "rank": 5,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Medical agent with ToolUniverse (211 biomedical tools), ToolGen (multi-agent tool construction from API docs), and ToolRAG (adaptive tool retrieval). Achieves 92.1% accuracy on drug reasoning",
      "usage": "Key reference for automated medical tool construction (ToolGen) and domain-specific tool universe design",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10970"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MCP-Zero: Active Tool Discovery for Autonomous LLM Agents",
      "rank": 6,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Active agent framework enabling LLMs to autonomously discover tools on-demand via MCP. Uses hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools with 98% token reduction",
      "usage": "Reference for MCP-based active tool discovery and iterative capability extension",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.01056"
    },
    {
      "reference": "HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face",
      "rank": 7,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Foundational work on LLM-as-controller connecting to expert models via API. Four-stage pipeline: task planning, model selection, task execution, response generation",
      "usage": "Foundational reference for wrapping ML models as callable tools for an LLM controller",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17580"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MedAgentGym: Training LLM Agents for Code-Based Medical Reasoning at Scale",
      "rank": 8,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "ICLR 2026 paper on training medical LLM agents with code-based tool use. Demonstrates SFT and DPO for medical agent training",
      "usage": "Reference for medical agent training methodology and code-based medical reasoning",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.04405"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning",
      "rank": 9,
      "type": "application",
      "justification": "Direct intersection of medical segmentation and agentic RL. Multi-turn interaction with MedSAM via reinforcement learning for iterative refinement",
      "usage": "Template for how Medical Agent invokes segmentation skills with iterative agentic refinement",
      "url": "https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent"
    }
  ],
  "task2": "Build a universal Medical Model-to-Skill wrapping pipeline and an LLM-powered Medical Agent that can discover, select, and compose Medical Skills for complex clinical workflows, targeting MICCAI 2026.\n\nCore Idea: Design a generic wrapping framework that can automatically convert any medical AI model (segmentation, classification, detection, registration, report generation, VQA, etc.) into a standardized Medical Skill using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the interoperability layer. The pipeline takes a medical model (with its code, weights, and API documentation) as input and produces a self-describing MCP-compatible Medical Skill as output — complete with structured input/output schemas, medical metadata (modality, anatomy, task type), safety constraints, and validation checks.\n\nKey Components:\n(1) Med-SkillGen: A universal wrapping pipeline (inspired by TxAgent's ToolGen) that auto-generates MCP-compatible Medical Skills from existing medical AI models. This includes: (a) automatic API analysis and schema extraction from model code/documentation, (b) medical metadata annotation (imaging modality, anatomical region, clinical task type, safety level), (c) standardized I/O interface generation following MCP tool specification, and (d) validation test generation to ensure skill correctness.\n(2) Med-SkillLib: A curated Medical Skills library built by applying Med-SkillGen to existing open-source medical models (MedSAM, MedSAM2, CheXagent, etc.) and existing Medical MCP servers (DICOM-MCP, healthcare-mcp-public, medical-calculator-MCP, etc.). The library organizes skills hierarchically by modality, anatomy, and task type.\n(3) Med-Agent: An LLM-powered agent that uses active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) to dynamically retrieve and compose Medical Skills from Med-SkillLib for multi-step clinical workflows. The agent performs task planning, skill selection via hierarchical semantic routing, execution graph construction, and result integration.\n\nThe key innovation bridges two worlds: (a) general-purpose agent skill frameworks (ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL, MCP-Zero) that lack medical domain expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools that lack interoperability and composability. By using MCP as the universal protocol, any medical model can be 'plugged in' as a skill, enabling the agent to solve complex clinical scenarios (e.g., 'Given this chest CT, segment the lungs, detect nodules, measure their size, assess malignancy risk, and generate a structured report') through dynamic skill composition.\n\nThis aligns with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by making individual medical AI algorithms composable and deployable through a unified agent framework.",
  "task1": ""
}

      "usage": "Reference for skill organization, retrieval, and pipeline composition architecture",
      "url": "https://github.com/ynulihao/AgentSkillOS"
    },
    {
      "reference": "SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance",
      "rank": 4,
      "type": "evaluation",
      "justification": "First benchmark for evaluating agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains (86 tasks, 7300+ trials). Shows +16pp average, +51.9pp in healthcare",
      "usage": "Evidence that skills are especially beneficial in healthcare; evaluation methodology reference",
      "url": "https://www.skillsbench.ai/"
    },
    {
      "reference": "TxAgent: An AI Agent for Therapeutic Reasoning Across a Universe of Tools",
      "rank": 5,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Medical agent with ToolUniverse (211 biomedical tools), ToolGen (multi-agent tool construction from API docs), and ToolRAG (adaptive tool retrieval). Achieves 92.1% accuracy on drug reasoning",
      "usage": "Key reference for automated medical tool construction (ToolGen) and domain-specific tool universe design",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10970"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MCP-Zero: Active Tool Discovery for Autonomous LLM Agents",
      "rank": 6,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Active agent framework enabling LLMs to autonomously discover tools on-demand via MCP. Uses hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools with 98% token reduction",
      "usage": "Reference for MCP-based active tool discovery and iterative capability extension",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.01056"
    },
    {
      "reference": "HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face",
      "rank": 7,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "Foundational work on LLM-as-controller connecting to expert models via API. Four-stage pipeline: task planning, model selection, task execution, response generation",
      "usage": "Foundational reference for wrapping ML models as callable tools for an LLM controller",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17580"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MedAgentGym: Training LLM Agents for Code-Based Medical Reasoning at Scale",
      "rank": 8,
      "type": "methodology",
      "justification": "ICLR 2026 paper on training medical LLM agents with code-based tool use. Demonstrates SFT and DPO for medical agent training",
      "usage": "Reference for medical agent training methodology and code-based medical reasoning",
      "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.04405"
    },
    {
      "reference": "MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning",
      "rank": 9,
      "type": "application",
      "justification": "Direct intersection of medical segmentation and agentic RL. Multi-turn interaction with MedSAM via reinforcement learning for iterative refinement",
      "usage": "Template for how Medical Agent invokes segmentation skills with iterative agentic refinement",
      "url": "https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent"
    }
  ],
  "task2": "Build a universal Medical Model-to-Skill wrapping pipeline and an LLM-powered Medical Agent that can discover, select, and compose Medical Skills for complex clinical workflows, targeting MICCAI 2026.\n\nCore Idea: Design a generic wrapping framework that can automatically convert any medical AI model (segmentation, classification, detection, registration, report generation, VQA, etc.) into a standardized Medical Skill using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the interoperability layer. The pipeline takes a medical model (with its code, weights, and API documentation) as input and produces a self-describing MCP-compatible Medical Skill as output — complete with structured input/output schemas, medical metadata (modality, anatomy, task type), safety constraints, and validation checks.\n\nKey Components:\n(1) Med-SkillGen: A universal wrapping pipeline (inspired by TxAgent's ToolGen) that auto-generates MCP-compatible Medical Skills from existing medical AI models. This includes: (a) automatic API analysis and schema extraction from model code/documentation, (b) medical metadata annotation (imaging modality, anatomical region, clinical task type, safety level), (c) standardized I/O interface generation following MCP tool specification, and (d) validation test generation to ensure skill correctness.\n(2) Med-SkillLib: A curated Medical Skills library built by applying Med-SkillGen to existing open-source medical models (MedSAM, MedSAM2, CheXagent, etc.) and existing Medical MCP servers (DICOM-MCP, healthcare-mcp-public, medical-calculator-MCP, etc.). The library organizes skills hierarchically by modality, anatomy, and task type.\n(3) Med-Agent: An LLM-powered agent that uses active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) to dynamically retrieve and compose Medical Skills from Med-SkillLib for multi-step clinical workflows. The agent performs task planning, skill selection via hierarchical semantic routing, execution graph construction, and result integration.\n\nThe key innovation bridges two worlds: (a) general-purpose agent skill frameworks (ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL, MCP-Zero) that lack medical domain expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools that lack interoperability and composability. By using MCP as the universal protocol, any medical model can be 'plugged in' as a skill, enabling the agent to solve complex clinical scenarios (e.g., 'Given this chest CT, segment the lungs, detect nodules, measure their size, assess malignancy risk, and generate a structured report') through dynamic skill composition.\n\nThis aligns with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by making individual medical AI algorithms composable and deployable through a unified agent framework.",
  "task1": ""
}

-  "task2": "Build a Medical Skills library and Agent framework for medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention tasks, targeting MICCAI 2026. The project aims to: (1) Collect and curate existing open-source medical AI models and tools (e.g., segmentation models like MedSAM, classification models, detection models, registration models, report generation models) and encapsulate them as standardized, callable Medical Skills; (2) Design new skill modules to fill gaps in the medical skill ecosystem; (3) Build a Medical Agent powered by a large language model that can automatically discover, select, and compose appropriate Medical Skills to solve complex, multi-step medical tasks (e.g., a clinical workflow involving image preprocessing, organ segmentation, lesion detection, measurement, and report generation). The framework should support hierarchical skill organization, intelligent skill retrieval based on task requirements, and dynamic skill composition via execution graphs. The key innovation lies in bridging the gap between general-purpose agent skill frameworks (like ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL) and the specialized requirements of the medical imaging domain, where skills require domain-specific validation, safety constraints, and multi-modal data handling. The project should align with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by demonstrating practical clinical utility.",

+  "task2": "Build a universal Medical Model-to-Skill wrapping pipeline and an LLM-powered Medical Agent that can discover, select, and compose Medical Skills for complex clinical workflows, targeting MICCAI 2026.

Core Idea: Design a generic wrapping framework that can automatically convert any medical AI model (segmentation, classification, detection, registration, report generation, VQA, etc.) into a standardized Medical Skill using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the interoperability layer. The pipeline takes a medical model (with its code, weights, and API documentation) as input and produces a self-describing MCP-compatible Medical Skill as output — complete with structured input/output schemas, medical metadata (modality, anatomy, task type), safety constraints, and validation checks.

Key Components:
(1) Med-SkillGen: A universal wrapping pipeline (inspired by TxAgent's ToolGen) that auto-generates MCP-compatible Medical Skills from existing medical AI models. This includes: (a) automatic API analysis and schema extraction from model code/documentation, (b) medical metadata annotation (imaging modality, anatomical region, clinical task type, safety level), (c) standardized I/O interface generation following MCP tool specification, and (d) validation test generation to ensure skill correctness.
(2) Med-SkillLib: A curated Medical Skills library built by applying Med-SkillGen to existing open-source medical models (MedSAM, MedSAM2, CheXagent, etc.) and existing Medical MCP servers (DICOM-MCP, healthcare-mcp-public, medical-calculator-MCP, etc.). The library organizes skills hierarchically by modality, anatomy, and task type.
(3) Med-Agent: An LLM-powered agent that uses active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) to dynamically retrieve and compose Medical Skills from Med-SkillLib for multi-step clinical workflows. The agent performs task planning, skill selection via hierarchical semantic routing, execution graph construction, and result integration.

The key innovation bridges two worlds: (a) general-purpose agent skill frameworks (ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL, MCP-Zero) that lack medical domain expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools that lack interoperability and composability. By using MCP as the universal protocol, any medical model can be 'plugged in' as a skill, enabling the agent to solve complex clinical scenarios (e.g., 'Given this chest CT, segment the lungs, detect nodules, measure their size, assess malignancy risk, and generate a structured report') through dynamic skill composition.

This aligns with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by making individual medical AI algorithms composable and deployable through a unified agent framework.",

"references": "1. [Rank 1, methodology] SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery (arXiv:2602.08234) — Core framework for automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical skill library construction from agent experiences. Uses experience-based distillation, hierarchical SKILLBANK organization, and recursive evolution during RL.
2. [Rank 2, methodology] ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs (arXiv:2307.16789, ICLR'24 Spotlight) — General tool-use framework with ToolBench dataset, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning. Established architecture for LLM tool integration at scale.
3. [Rank 3, methodology] AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem — OS for discovering, composing, and running skill pipelines from 90K+ skills. Uses skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.
4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains (86 tasks, 7300+ trials). Shows +16pp average improvement, +51.9pp in healthcare. Optimal design uses 2-3 focused skills.",

"references": "1. [Rank 1, methodology] SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery (arXiv:2602.08234) — Automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical SKILLBANK organization via recursive RL evolution.
2. [Rank 2, methodology] ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs (arXiv:2307.16789, ICLR'24 Spotlight) — Tool-use framework with ToolBench, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning.
3. [Rank 3, methodology] AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem — 90K+ skills via skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.
4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains. +51.9pp in healthcare.
5. [Rank 5, methodology] TxAgent: An AI Agent for Therapeutic Reasoning Across a Universe of Tools (arXiv:2503.10970) — ToolUniverse (211 tools), ToolGen (auto tool construction from API docs), ToolRAG (adaptive retrieval). 92.1% accuracy.
6. [Rank 6, methodology] MCP-Zero: Active Tool Discovery for Autonomous LLM Agents (arXiv:2506.01056) — Active MCP-based tool discovery with hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools. 98% token reduction.

Uses skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.\n4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains (86 tasks, 7300+ trials). Shows +16pp average improvement, +51.9pp in healthcare. Optimal design uses 2-3 focused skills.",
  "ideas": "",
  "dataset_description": "Custom medical imaging domain. The project spans multiple medical imaging tasks (segmentation, classification, detection, registration, report generation, etc.). Evaluation will involve assembling Medical Skills into an agent framework and testing on established medical imaging benchmarks. Specific datasets to be determined during the idea generation and code survey phases. Candidate public datasets include: medical image segmentation benchmarks (BTCV, AMOS, TotalSegmentator), classification benchmarks (CheXpert, ISIC), VQA benchmarks (VQA-RAD, PathVQA), and clinical report generation benchmarks (MIMIC-CXR, IU X-Ray). The evaluation should measure both individual skill performance and the agent's ability to compose skills for complex multi-step clinical workflows."
}

lines:
-  "references": "1. [Rank 1, methodology] SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery (arXiv:2602.08234) — Core framework for automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical skill library construction from agent experiences. Uses experience-based distillation, hierarchical SKILLBANK organization, and recursive evolution during RL.
2. [Rank 2, methodology] ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs (arXiv:2307.16789, ICLR'24 Spotlight) — General tool-use framework with ToolBench dataset, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning. Established architecture for LLM tool integration at scale.
3. [Rank 3, methodology] AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem — OS for discovering, composing, and running skill pipelines from 90K+ skills. Uses skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.
4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains (86 tasks, 7300+ trials). Shows +16pp average improvement, +51.9pp in healthcare. Optimal design uses 2-3 focused skills.",

+  "references": "1. [Rank 1, methodology] SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery (arXiv:2602.08234) — Automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical SKILLBANK organization via recursive RL evolution.
2. [Rank 2, methodology] ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs (arXiv:2307.16789, ICLR'24 Spotlight) — Tool-use framework with ToolBench, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning.
3. [Rank 3, methodology] AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem — 90K+ skills via skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.
4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains. +51.9pp in healthcare.
5. [Rank 5, methodology] TxAgent: An AI Agent for Therapeutic Reasoning Across a Universe of Tools (arXiv:2503.10970) — ToolUniverse (211 tools), ToolGen (auto tool construction from API docs), ToolRAG (adaptive retrieval). 92.1% accuracy.
6. [Rank 6, methodology] MCP-Zero: Active Tool Discovery for Autonomous LLM Agents (arXiv:2506.01056) — Active MCP-based tool discovery with hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools. 98% token reduction.
7. [Rank 7, methodology] HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face (arXiv:2303.17580) — LLM-as-controller wrapping ML models: task planning → model selection → execution → response.

9. [Rank 9, application] MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic RL — Agentic interaction with MedSAM via multi-turn RL.",

content.text:
所有文件已更新完毕。以下是更新后的研究方向总结：

---

## 更新后的任务描述

### 核心转变：统一的 Medical Model → Skill 包装流程

原来的 (1) 收集已有工具 和 (2) 设计新模块 合并为一个**通用包装管线**的概念：

### 三大组件

| 组件 | 描述 | 灵感来源 |
|------|------|---------|
| **Med-SkillGen** | 通用包装管线：自动将任意医学AI模型转换为MCP兼容的标准化Medical Skill | TxAgent的ToolGen（自动工具构建） |
| **Med-SkillLib** | 医学技能库：通过Med-SkillGen包装已有模型（MedSAM, CheXagent等）+ 直接集成已有Medical MCP Server（DICOM-MCP, healthcare-mcp等） | AgentSkillOS的层级化技能树 |
| **Med-Agent** | LLM驱动的Agent：主动发现、选择和组合Medical Skills完成多步骤临床任务 | MCP-Zero的主动工具发现 |

### 新增的关键参考资源

| 论文/项目 | 贡献 | Stars |
|-----------|------|-------|
| [TxAgent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.10970) | ToolGen（自动从API文档构建工具）+ ToolUniverse（211个生物医学工具）| 596★ |
| [MCP-Zero](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.01056) | 基于MCP的主动工具发现，层级语义路由，2797工具，98%token减少 | 446★ |
| [HuggingGPT/JARVIS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17580) | LLM-as-controller包装ML模型的奠基工作 | 23600+★ |
| [MedAgentGym](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.04405) | ICLR'26, 医学Agent代码推理训练 | 78★ |
| [healthcare-mcp-public](https://github.com/Cicatriiz/healthcare-mcp-public) | 已有Medical MCP Server（FDA, PubMed, DICOM, ICD-10等） | 87★ |
| [dicom-mcp](https://github.com/ChristianHinge/dicom-mcp) | DICOM MCP Server（查询、PDF提取、C-MOVE） | - |
| [awesome-medical-mcp-servers](https://github.com/sunanhe/awesome-medical-mcp-servers) | Medical MCP Server合集 | - |

### 创新点

**桥接两个世界**：
- **通用Agent框架**（ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL, MCP-Zero）→ 缺乏医学领域知识
- **孤立的医学AI工具**（MedSAM, CheXagent, DICOM-MCP等）→ 缺乏互操作性和可组合性

通过 **MCP作为统一协议** + **Med-SkillGen作为通用包装器**，任何医学模型都可以"即插即用"为一个Skill，使Agent能解决复杂临床场景。

---

Base directory for this skill: /home/dingjie/workspace/ai-scientist/workspace/Med-Skills/.claude/skills/inno-idea-generation

# Inno Idea Generation

## Directory structure

```
skills/inno-idea-generation/
├── SKILL.md                                    ← this file
├── prompts/
│   ├── build_idea_query.md                     ← Step 1 prompt template & parameter docs
│   ├── new_idea_prompt.md                      ← Step 2 diversity-loop prompt template
│   └── build_idea_selection_query.md           ← Step 3 selection prompt template & post-processing
└── references/
    └── idea_agent_instructions.md              ← Full Idea Agent system prompt & tool list
```

> **How to use the resource files**: Each prompt template in `prompts/` documents
> the exact parameters, the full text template, and usage notes (when it is a new
> conversation vs. appended message, how to format multi-idea lists, etc.).
> The `references/` directory contains the Idea Agent's complete system instructions
> including its hard constraints, conciseness rules, dual-mode workflow, and
> selection rubric. Consult these files for the authoritative details; the steps
> below provide a summary.

## Inputs

| Parameter          | Required | Description |
|--------------------|----------|-------------|
| `data_module`      | Yes      | The imported metaprompt module (provides `TASK` field describing the ML task) |
| `references`       | Yes      | Pre-formatted string listing all source papers |
| `prepare_res`      | Yes      | Full text response from the Prepare Agent (selected repositories and reasoning) |
| `download_res`     | Yes      | Result log from downloading arXiv paper sources |
| `context_variables`| Yes      | Shared context dictionary |

## Outputs

| Output                | Description |
|-----------------------|-------------|
| `raw_ideas`           | List of all generated raw idea texts |
| `selected_idea`       | The best idea chosen by the selection step |
| `context_variables`   | Updated with `idea_generation_history` (list) and `final_selected_idea_data` (dict) |

## Cache file outputs

Each step produces **two kinds** of files:

1. **`.txt` files** (primary) — the full markdown content of each idea, written directly to `Ideation/ideas/`
2. **`.json` files** (derived) — structured metadata under `Ideation/ideas/logs/`, whose `content` fields **must be copied verbatim** from the corresponding `.txt` files (never summarized)

### Full directory layout

```
Ideation/ideas/
├── idea_query_round_1.txt                    ← Step 1: the query prompt sent to the agent
├── raw_idea_1.txt                            ← Step 1: full markdown of first idea
├── raw_idea_2.txt                            ← Step 2: full markdown of second idea
├── raw_idea_3.txt                            ← Step 2: full markdown of third idea
├── selected_idea.txt                         ← Step 3: full markdown of final selected idea
└── logs/
    ├── idea_generation_agent.json            ← Step 1: metadata + full text from raw_idea_1.txt
    ├── idea_generation_agent_iter_1.json     ← Step 2: metadata + full text from raw_idea_2.txt
    ├── idea_generation_agent_iter_2.json     ← Step 2: metadata + full text from raw_idea_3.txt
    └── idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json ← Step 3: metadata + full text from selected_idea.txt
```

### Write order (critical)

For every step, **always write the `.txt` file first**, then build the `.json` file by copying the `.txt` content into the appropriate field:

1. Write `raw_idea_{N}.txt` with the agent's full response
2. Read it back (or keep in memory) and embed the full text into `idea_generation_history[N-1].content`
3. Write the corresponding `agents/*.json`

For the selection step:
1. Write `selected_idea.txt` with the agent's full selection response
2. Copy that full text into `final_selected_idea_data.selected_idea_text`
3. Write `agents/idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json`

### `.txt` file naming

| Step | File name | Content |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Query prompt | `idea_query_round_1.txt` | The `build_idea_query` prompt sent in Step 1 |
| Round 1 idea | `raw_idea_1.txt` | Full markdown of the first idea |
| Round N idea | `raw_idea_{N}.txt` | Full markdown of the Nth idea |
| Final selection | `selected_idea.txt` | Full markdown of the selected & refined idea |

### `.json` file naming

| Step | File name | `iter_times` value |
|------|-----------|--------------------|
| First idea | `idea_generation_agent.json` | (none / default) |
| Round N+1 idea | `idea_generation_agent_iter_{N}.json` | `N` (1-indexed: iter_1 = round 2, iter_2 = round 3, ...) |
| Selection | `idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json` | `"select"` |

### `.json` file format

Each file contains `context_variables` only (no messages). The `content` fields hold the **full text** copied from the corresponding `.txt` files:

```json
{
  "context_variables": {
    "ideas_path": "<ideas_path>",
    "references_path": "<references_path>",
    "date_limit": "YYYY-MM-DD",
    "prepare_result": { ... },
    "idea_generation_history": [
      { "round": 1, "status": "raw", "content": "<FULL text from raw_idea_1.txt>" },
      { "round": 2, "status": "raw", "content": "<FULL text from raw_idea_2.txt>" }
    ],
    "notes": [],
    "final_selected_idea_data": {
      "raw_idea": "<title of best candidate>",
      "selected_idea_text": "<FULL text from selected_idea.txt>"
    }
  }
}
```

- `idea_generation_history` grows with each file — the first file has 1 entry, iter_1 has 2 entries, etc.
- `final_selected_idea_data` is only present in `idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json`.
- **IMPORTANT**: `content` and `selected_idea_text` must contain the **complete** markdown from the `.txt` file — never a summary or abbreviation.

## Step-by-step Instructions

### Step 1 — Generate the first idea

> Full template & parameter docs: [`prompts/build_idea_query.md`](prompts/build_idea_query.md)
> Agent system prompt: [`references/idea_agent_instructions.md`](references/idea_agent_instructions.md)

Build the idea query from four components:

```
I have a task related to machine learning:
{data_module.TASK}
And a list of papers for your reference:
{references}

I have carefully gone through these papers' github repositories and found
download some of them in my local machine, with the following information:
{prepare_res}
And I have also downloaded the corresponding paper (LaTeX sources, markdown,
txt), with the following information:
{download_res}

Your task is to thoroughly review research papers and generate innovative
ideas for the given task.

Note that the math formula should be as complete as possible.
```

Send this to the **Idea Agent**. Record the response as the first `raw_idea` and append to `raw_ideas`.

**Save (txt first, then json)**:
1. Write the query prompt → `Ideation/ideas/idea_query_round_1.txt`
2. Write the agent's full response → `Ideation/ideas/raw_idea_1.txt`
3. Initialize `context_variables["idea_generation_history"]` and append:
   ```json
   { "round": 1, "status": "raw", "content": "<FULL text from raw_idea_1.txt>" }
   ```
4. Write → `Ideation/ideas/logs/idea_generation_agent.json`

### Step 2 — Generate additional ideas (diversity loop)

> Full template & constraint rationale: [`prompts/new_idea_prompt.md`](prompts/new_idea_prompt.md)

Repeat `DEFAULT_IDEA_NUM - 1` more times (default: 2 more rounds, for a total of 3 ideas).

For each round, build a follow-up prompt that includes all previously generated ideas and strict constraints:

```
Here are the ideas from previous rounds:

--- Candidate Idea #1 ---
<raw_idea_1>

--- Candidate Idea #2 ---
<raw_idea_2>
...

Please survey again and give me another idea that is different from all
ideas above. You MUST obey these hard constraints:
1) Do NOT introduce any new input modalities, new data sources, or new labels/targets.
2) Do NOT change the task, the disease/condition, the primary modality, or the prediction target.
```

Append the new idea prompt to the existing conversation and call the **Idea Agent** again. Record each new idea in `raw_ideas` and `idea_generation_history`.

**Save (txt first, then json)** after each round:
1. Write the agent's full response → `Ideation/ideas/raw_idea_{round}.txt` (e.g. `raw_idea_2.txt`, `raw_idea_3.txt`)
2. Append to `idea_generation_history`:
   ```json
   { "round": N, "status": "raw", "content": "<FULL text from raw_idea_{N}.txt>" }
   ```
3. Write → `Ideation/ideas/logs/idea_generation_agent_iter_{N-1}.json` (iter_1 for round 2, iter_2 for round 3, etc.)

### Step 3 — Select the best idea

> Full template, separator format & post-processing logic: [`prompts/build_idea_selection_query.md`](prompts/build_idea_selection_query.md)

Build the selection query:

```
You have generated {N} innovative ideas for the given task:
<idea_1>
===================
===================
<idea_2>
===================
===================
...

Your task is to analyze multiple existing ideas, select the most novel one,
enhance the idea if any key information is missing, finally give me the most
novel idea with refined math formula and code implementation.
Directly output the selected refined idea report.
```

Send this as a **new** conversation (not appended to the idea-generation thread) to the **Idea Agent**.

The agent's response is the `selected_idea`. To verify which raw idea was chosen, compare the response text against each entry in `raw_ideas` — if a raw idea's text appears as a substring (or the first 100 chars match), use the original raw idea as `best_candidate`. Otherwise use the agent's response as-is.

**Save (txt first, then json)**:
1. Write the agent's full selection response → `Ideation/ideas/selected_idea.txt`
2. Copy that full text into the context:
   ```json
   context_variables["final_selected_idea_data"] = {
     "raw_idea": "<title of best_candidate>",
     "selected_idea_text": "<FULL text from selected_idea.txt>"
   }
   ```
3. Write → `Ideation/ideas/logs/idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json`

## Configuration

| Constant             | Default | Description |
|----------------------|---------|-------------|
| `DEFAULT_IDEA_NUM`   | 3       | Total number of raw ideas to generate before selection |

## Checklist

- [ ] Query prompt saved → `idea_query_round_1.txt`
- [ ] First raw idea saved → `raw_idea_1.txt`, then full text copied into `logs/idea_generation_agent.json`
- [ ] Additional raw ideas saved → `raw_idea_{N}.txt`, then full text copied into `logs/idea_generation_agent_iter_{N-1}.json`
- [ ] `idea_generation_history` in `context_variables` contains **full text** (not summaries) for all rounds
- [ ] Best idea selected and saved → `selected_idea.txt`, then full text copied into `logs/idea_generation_agent_iter_select.json`
- [ ] `final_selected_idea_data.selected_idea_text` contains **full text** from `selected_idea.txt`
- [ ] All `.txt` files written to `Ideation/ideas/`, all `.json` files written to `Ideation/ideas/logs/`

# build_idea_query

Constructs the initial prompt for the first round of idea generation.

## Parameters

| Parameter          | Type   | Description |
|--------------------|--------|-------------|
| `task_description` | string | The ML task description (from `data_module.TASK`) |
| `references`       | string | Pre-formatted string listing all source papers |
| `prepare_res`      | string | Full response from Prepare Agent (selected repos and reasoning) |
| `download_res`     | string | Result log from downloading arXiv paper sources |

## Template

```
I have a task related to machine learning:
{task_description}
And a list of papers for your reference:
{references}

I have carefully gone through these papers' github repositories and found download some of them in my local machine, with the following information:
{prepare_res}
And I have also downloaded the corresponding paper (LaTeX sources, markdown, txt), with the following information:
{download_res}

Your task is to thoroughly review research papers and generate innovative ideas for the given task.

Note that the math formula should be as complete as possible.
```

## Usage

This is the **first message** sent to the Idea Agent. The agent will:
1. Read the referenced papers from `Ideation/references/papers/`
2. Analyze the codebase structure from `Experiment/code_references/<repo_name>/`
3. Generate an innovative idea with: Challenges, Existing Methods, Motivation, Proposed Method, Key Equations, Technical Details, Expected Outcomes

     2→
     3→Constructs the follow-up prompt for generating additional diverse ideas (rounds 2 through N).
     4→
     5→## Parameters
     6→
     7→| Parameter             | Type   | Description |
     8→|-----------------------|--------|-------------|
     9→| `previous_ideas_text` | string | All previously generated ideas, formatted as numbered candidates |
    10→
    11→## Building `previous_ideas_text`
    12→
    13→Iterate over all current `raw_ideas` and format:
    14→
    15→```
    16→--- Candidate Idea #1 ---
    17→<raw_idea_1>
    18→
    19→--- Candidate Idea #2 ---
    20→<raw_idea_2>
    21→
    22→...
    23→```
    24→
    25→## Template
    26→
    27→```
    28→Here are the ideas from previous rounds:
    29→{previous_ideas_text}
    30→
    31→Please survey again and give me another idea that is different from all ideas above. You MUST obey these hard constraints:
    32→1) Do NOT introduce any new input modalities, new data sources, or new labels/targets.

    34→```

# new_idea_prompt

Constructs the follow-up prompt for generating additional diverse ideas (rounds 2 through N).

## Parameters

| Parameter             | Type   | Description |
|-----------------------|--------|-------------|
| `previous_ideas_text` | string | All previously generated ideas, formatted as numbered candidates |

## Building `previous_ideas_text`

Iterate over all current `raw_ideas` and format:

```
--- Candidate Idea #1 ---
<raw_idea_1>

--- Candidate Idea #2 ---
<raw_idea_2>

...
```

## Template

```
Here are the ideas from previous rounds:
{previous_ideas_text}

Please survey again and give me another idea that is different from all ideas above. You MUST obey these hard constraints:
1) Do NOT introduce any new input modalities, new data sources, or new labels/targets.
2) Do NOT change the task, the disease/condition, the primary modality, or the prediction target.
```

## Usage

This prompt is **appended** to the existing conversation with the Idea Agent (not a new conversation). The agent continues the same chat thread and generates a new idea that is distinct from all previous ones.

The iteration uses `iter_times = i + 1` (where `i` is the 0-based loop index), which maps to the cache filename `idea_generation_agent_iter_{i+1}.json`.

## Constraint rationale

The two hard constraints prevent scope creep:
1. **No new modalities/data/labels** — the idea must work within the existing data pipeline
2. **No task/target changes** — ensures all ideas are comparable and address the same research problem

# build_idea_selection_query

Constructs the prompt for selecting the best idea from all generated candidates.

## Parameters

| Parameter  | Type         | Description |
|------------|--------------|-------------|
| `idea_num` | int          | Total number of generated ideas |
| `ideas`    | list[string] | List of all raw idea texts |

## Building the ideas text

Join all ideas with the double-separator:

```
separator = '\n===================\n===================\n'
ideas_text = separator.join(ideas)
```

## Template

```
You have generated {idea_num} innovative ideas for the given task:
{ideas_text}

Your task is to analyze multiple existing ideas, select the most novel one, enhance the idea if any key information is missing, finally give me the most novel idea with refined math formula and code implementation. Directly output the selected refined idea report.
```

## Usage

This is sent as a **new conversation** (fresh message list, not appended to the generation thread) to the Idea Agent with `iter_times = "select"`.

The agent uses its built-in rubric to evaluate:
- **Novelty**: is the core contribution genuinely new?
- **Contract-fit**: does it strictly match task/modality/target constraints?
- **Feasibility**: can it be implemented with available components?
- **Evidence alignment**: are claims supported by the provided papers?
- **Risk profile**: hidden dependencies or implicit new data requirements?

The agent selects one idea, optionally enhances it, and outputs the final report.

## Post-processing

After receiving the selection response:

1. Compare the response text against each entry in `raw_ideas`
2. If a raw idea's text appears as a substring (or the first 100 characters match), use the original raw idea as `best_candidate`
3. Otherwise, use the agent's response as-is (it may have been enhanced)

Store both the original and the selected version in `context_variables["final_selected_idea_data"]`.

file.content:
# Idea Generation Agent — System Instructions

This document describes the system prompt and behavioral contract of the Idea Generation Agent.

## Role

An agent specialized in analyzing academic papers and generating innovative research ideas.

## Path conventions

The agent works within the project directory using the new semantic layout:

| Path | Contents |
|------|----------|
| `Ideation/references/papers/` | Downloaded arXiv LaTeX sources (`.tex`, `.txt`, `.md`) |
| `Experiment/code_references/<repo_name>/` | Cloned GitHub repositories |

## Dual-mode Operation

The agent operates in one of two modes depending on the user message:

1. **New Idea Generation** — when given papers and a task description
2. **Idea Selection & Enhancement** — when given multiple existing ideas

## System Prompt

```
You are an `Idea Generation Agent` specialized in analyzing academic papers
located in `Ideation/references/papers/` and generating innovative ideas. Your task
is to either:
1. Thoroughly review research papers and generate comprehensive ideas for the
   given task, or
2. Analyze multiple existing ideas and select/enhance the most novel one.

OBJECTIVE:
For New Idea Generation:
- Conduct thorough literature review of provided papers
- Identify research gaps and challenges
- Generate innovative and feasible ideas (focus on 3-4 core innovative
  modules, avoid over-stacking)
- Provide detailed technical solutions

For Idea Selection & Enhancement:
- Analyze all provided ideas
- Select the most novel and promising idea based on:
  * Technical innovation
  * Potential impact
  * Feasibility
  * Completeness
- Enhance the selected idea into a comprehensive proposal

HARD CONSTRAINTS (MUST OBEY FOR BOTH GENERATION AND SELECTION):
- You MUST NOT introduce any:
  * New input modalities (e.g., OCT when only fundus images are provided,
    additional clinical metadata if not in the current dataset)
  * New external data sources (e.g., new cohorts, new hospitals, extra
    unlabeled datasets beyond what is explicitly allowed)
  * New labels or targets (e.g., predicting new diseases, new grading
    schemes, or extra auxiliary tasks that require new annotation)
- You MUST NOT change:
  * The primary task definition
  * The disease/condition of interest
  * The primary modality (e.g., if the task is fundus-based DR grading,
    you must stay on fundus-based DR grading)
  * The prediction target (e.g., DR severity level definition, or other
    specified target)
- You MAY derive additional signals ONLY from the existing data and labels,
  for example:
  * Training additional models on the same data
  * Generating Grad-CAM / attention maps / feature attributions
  * Self-supervised or auxiliary objectives that do NOT require new labels
    or new modalities
- Any idea that violates the above constraints MUST be discarded.

ADDITIONAL CONSTRAINTS FOR CONCISENESS (MUST OBEY):
1. Innovation Focus: Limit core innovative modules to 3-4 (avoid more
   than 4). Ensure modules have clear synergistic logic (e.g., "A solves X,
   B enhances A's effect on Y") instead of independent stacking.
2. Expression Granularity:
   - Avoid redundant implementation details (e.g., repeated mentions of
     mixed precision/gradient checkpointing, full PyTorch class definitions
     with trivial code).
   - Code-related content: Only provide key algorithm skeletons (pseudocode
     for core forward logic of innovative modules).
   - Mathematical formulations: Retain core formulas (e.g., key loss
     functions, module forward equations) and omit repetitive derivation.
3. Structure Streamlining: Each section (Challenges, Existing Methods,
   Proposed Method, etc.) should focus on core logic with no redundancy
   between sections.

AVAILABLE TOOLS (standard Linux commands):
1. Paper Navigation:
   - cat / less / head / tail: Read paper files
   - sed -n 'START,ENDp': Read specific line ranges
   - tree / ls / find: List and browse files and directories
2. Content Search:
   - grep / rg (ripgrep): Search for text in files or across directories
   - wc -l: Get file line counts for overview

WORKFLOW:
1. Task Identification:
   - If given papers: Proceed with literature review
   - If given multiple ideas: Proceed with idea selection & enhancement

2. For Literature Review:
   - Thoroughly read and analyze all provided papers
   - Extract key concepts, methods, and results
   - Identify research trends and gaps

3. For Idea Selection:
   - Analyze all provided ideas using rubric:
     * Novelty: genuinely new vs. common baselines?
     * Contract-fit: strictly matches task/modality/target constraints?
     * Feasibility: implementable with realistic components?
     * Evidence alignment: supported by provided papers?
     * Risk profile: hidden dependencies or implicit new data?
   - Select the single best idea and enhance it
   - State WHY it was chosen using the rubric criteria

4. Idea Generation/Enhancement:
   Generate a comprehensive proposal including:
   a) Challenges — current limitations and key bottlenecks
   b) Existing Methods — current approaches and their limitations
   c) Motivation — why the problem is important, what gaps to address
   d) Proposed Method — detailed technical solution with:
      - Step-by-step methodology
      - Mathematical formulations
      - Key innovations and improvements
      - Implementation considerations
      - Potential challenges and solutions
   e) Technical Details — architecture, algorithms, data flow
   f) Expected Outcomes — anticipated improvements, evaluation metrics

REQUIREMENTS:
- Be comprehensive in analysis
- Ensure ideas are novel yet feasible
- Provide detailed technical specifications
- Include mathematical formulations when relevant
- Make clear connections between challenges and solutions
```

## Tool List (generic Linux commands)

Since we do not have custom-configured tools, the agent should use standard
Linux commands available in the terminal to accomplish the same tasks:

| Action | Command | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| List files in papers directory | `ls`, `find`, `tree` | `ls Ideation/references/papers/` |
| Read a paper file | `cat`, `less`, `head`, `tail` | `cat Ideation/references/papers/paper1.tex` |
| Search for text in a file | `grep`, `rg` (ripgrep) | `grep -n "attention" Ideation/references/papers/paper1.tex` |
| Search across all papers | `grep -r`, `rg` | `rg "transformer" Ideation/references/papers/` |
| View file structure | `tree`, `ls -R` | `tree Ideation/ -L 2` |
| Read specific line range | `sed -n` | `sed -n '100,200p' Ideation/references/papers/paper1.tex` |
| Count lines / get overview | `wc -l` | `wc -l Ideation/references/papers/*.tex` |
| View repository structure | `tree`, `find` | `tree Experiment/code_references/repo_name/ -L 3` |

## Notes

- Paper files: `Ideation/references/papers/*.tex` (LaTeX sources downloaded by inno-prepare-resources)
- Cloned repositories: `Experiment/code_references/<repo_name>/` (e.g. `Experiment/code_references/BioASQ-QA/`)
- All file operations use standard Linux commands — no custom tool binaries are required

input.content:
I have a task related to machine learning:
Build a universal Medical Model-to-Skill wrapping pipeline and an LLM-powered Medical Agent that can discover, select, and compose Medical Skills for complex clinical workflows, targeting MICCAI 2026.

Core Idea: Design a generic wrapping framework that can automatically convert any medical AI model (segmentation, classification, detection, registration, report generation, VQA, etc.) into a standardized Medical Skill using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the interoperability layer. The pipeline takes a medical model (with its code, weights, and API documentation) as input and produces a self-describing MCP-compatible Medical Skill as output — complete with structured input/output schemas, medical metadata (modality, anatomy, task type), safety constraints, and validation checks.

Key Components:
(1) Med-SkillGen: A universal wrapping pipeline (inspired by TxAgent's ToolGen) that auto-generates MCP-compatible Medical Skills from existing medical AI models. This includes: (a) automatic API analysis and schema extraction from model code/documentation, (b) medical metadata annotation (imaging modality, anatomical region, clinical task type, safety level), (c) standardized I/O interface generation following MCP tool specification, and (d) validation test generation to ensure skill correctness.
(2) Med-SkillLib: A curated Medical Skills library built by applying Med-SkillGen to existing open-source medical models (MedSAM, MedSAM2, CheXagent, etc.) and existing Medical MCP servers (DICOM-MCP, healthcare-mcp-public, medical-calculator-MCP, etc.). The library organizes skills hierarchically by modality, anatomy, and task type.
(3) Med-Agent: An LLM-powered agent that uses active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) to dynamically retrieve and compose Medical Skills from Med-SkillLib for multi-step clinical workflows. The agent performs task planning, skill selection via hierarchical semantic routing, execution graph construction, and result integration.

The key innovation bridges two worlds: (a) general-purpose agent skill frameworks (ToolLLM, AgentSkillOS, SkillRL, MCP-Zero) that lack medical domain expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools that lack interoperability and composability. By using MCP as the universal protocol, any medical model can be 'plugged in' as a skill, enabling the agent to solve complex clinical scenarios through dynamic skill composition.

This aligns with MICCAI 2026's theme 'From Algorithms to Clinical Translation' by making individual medical AI algorithms composable and deployable through a unified agent framework.

And a list of papers for your reference:
1. [Rank 1, methodology] SkillRL: Skill-Based Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents with Automatic Skill Discovery (arXiv:2602.08234) — Automatic skill abstraction and hierarchical SKILLBANK organization via recursive RL evolution.
2. [Rank 2, methodology] ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs (arXiv:2307.16789, ICLR'24 Spotlight) — Tool-use framework with ToolBench, neural API retriever (ToolRetriever), and DFSDT reasoning.
3. [Rank 3, methodology] AgentSkillOS: Leveraging, Managing, and Scaling the Agent Skill Ecosystem — 90K+ skills via skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, and DAG-based orchestration.
4. [Rank 4, evaluation] SkillsBench: Evaluating How Agent Skills Improve AI Agent Performance — First benchmark for agent skill effectiveness across 11 domains. +51.9pp in healthcare.
5. [Rank 5, methodology] TxAgent: An AI Agent for Therapeutic Reasoning Across a Universe of Tools (arXiv:2503.10970) — ToolUniverse (211 tools), ToolGen (auto tool construction from API docs), ToolRAG (adaptive retrieval). 92.1% accuracy.
6. [Rank 6, methodology] MCP-Zero: Active Tool Discovery for Autonomous LLM Agents (arXiv:2506.01056) — Active MCP-based tool discovery with hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools. 98% token reduction.
7. [Rank 7, methodology] HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face (arXiv:2303.17580) — LLM-as-controller wrapping ML models: task planning → model selection → execution → response.
8. [Rank 8, methodology] MedAgentGym: Training LLM Agents for Code-Based Medical Reasoning at Scale (ICLR 2026) — Medical agent training with SFT and DPO for code-based reasoning.
9. [Rank 9, application] MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic RL — Agentic interaction with MedSAM via multi-turn RL.

I have carefully gone through these papers' github repositories and found download some of them in my local machine, with the following information:
Reference codebases selected:
- aiming-lab/SkillRL (276★) — Skill discovery and hierarchical organization via RL
- OpenBMB/ToolBench (5524★) — ICLR'24 spotlight, tool learning platform with ToolRetriever and DFSDT
- ynulihao/AgentSkillOS (128★) — 90K+ skills via skill retrieval and DAG orchestration
- mims-harvard/TxAgent (596★) — Therapeutic agent with ToolGen (auto tool construction) and 211 biomedical tools
- xfey/MCP-Zero (446★) — Active MCP-based tool discovery with hierarchical semantic routing
- microsoft/JARVIS (23600+★) — LLM-as-controller connecting to expert models (HuggingGPT)
- wshi83/MedAgentGym (78★) — ICLR'26, medical agent training with code-based reasoning
- CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent (30★) — Agentic RL for interactive medical image segmentation

- bowang-lab/MedSAM2 (542★) — 3D medical image and video segmentation
- Stanford-AIMI/CheXagent (213★) — Foundation model for chest X-ray interpretation
- Cicatriiz/healthcare-mcp-public (87★) — MCP server with FDA, PubMed, DICOM, ICD-10, medical calculator
- ChristianHinge/dicom-mcp — DICOM MCP server for PACS interactions
- sunanhe/awesome-medical-mcp-servers — Collection of Medical MCP servers

And I have also downloaded the corresponding paper (LaTeX sources, markdown, txt), with the following information:
Downloaded 2/4 papers successfully.

1. SkillRL (arXiv:2602.08234) — SUCCESS: PDF (13MB) + LaTeX source extracted (ICML 2026 submission)
2. ToolLLM (arXiv:2307.16789) — SUCCESS: PDF (2MB) + LaTeX source extracted (ICLR'24 spotlight)

input.prompt:
You are an Idea Generation Agent specialized in analyzing academic papers and generating innovative ideas for machine learning research.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- You MUST NOT introduce any new input modalities, new data sources, or new labels/targets beyond what is described.
- You MUST NOT change the primary task definition (building a Medical Skills framework using MCP for medical imaging agents).
- You MAY derive additional signals from existing data and methods.
- Focus on 3-4 core innovative modules (avoid over-stacking).
- Code: only key algorithm skeletons (pseudocode for core forward logic).
- Math: retain core formulas, omit repetitive derivation.

CONTEXT - You have access to these papers in Ideation/references/papers/:
- SkillRL (arXiv:2602.08234) — ICML 2026: Automatic skill abstraction via recursive RL. Key concepts: SKILLBANK (hierarchical skill library), experience-based distillation, skill evolution during RL training.
- ToolLLM (arXiv:2307.16789) — ICLR'24 Spotlight: ToolBench (16000+ APIs), ToolRetriever (neural API retrieval via sentence-BERT), DFSDT (depth-first search-based decision tree reasoning).

Additional references (no LaTeX source available):
- AgentSkillOS: 90K+ skills, skill tree construction, LLM-guided retrieval, DAG orchestration.
- SkillsBench: Agent skill benchmark, 86 tasks, 11 domains. +51.9pp in healthcare. Optimal: 2-3 focused skills.
- TxAgent: ToolUniverse (211 biomedical tools), ToolGen (multi-agent auto tool construction from API docs), ToolRAG (adaptive retrieval). 92.1% accuracy on drug reasoning.
- MCP-Zero: Active tool discovery via MCP. Hierarchical semantic routing across 2797 tools. 98% token reduction. Iterative capability extension.
- HuggingGPT/JARVIS: LLM-as-controller. 4-stage: task planning → model selection → execution → response.
- MedAgentGym: ICLR 2026. Training medical LLM agents with code-based tool use. SFT + DPO.
- MedSAM-Agent: Multi-turn agentic RL for interactive medical image segmentation with MedSAM.

Reference codebases:
- aiming-lab/SkillRL (276★), OpenBMB/ToolBench (5524★), ynulihao/AgentSkillOS (128★)
- mims-harvard/TxAgent (596★), xfey/MCP-Zero (446★), microsoft/JARVIS (23600+★)
- wshi83/MedAgentGym (78★), CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent (30★)
- bowang-lab/MedSAM (4071★), bowang-lab/MedSAM2 (542★), Stanford-AIMI/CheXagent (213★)
- Cicatriiz/healthcare-mcp-public (87★), ChristianHinge/dicom-mcp, sunanhe/awesome-medical-mcp-servers

Medical MCP servers already exist:
- DICOM-MCP (query PACS, PDF extraction, C-MOVE)
- healthcare-mcp-public (FDA drug info, PubMed, medRxiv, NCBI, clinical trials, ICD-10, DICOM metadata, medical calculator)
- mcp-slicer (3D Slicer integration)
- medical-calculator-MCP

TASK:
Build a universal Medical Model-to-Skill wrapping pipeline and an LLM-powered Medical Agent that can discover, select, and compose Medical Skills for complex clinical workflows, targeting MICCAI 2026.

Key Components:
(1) Med-SkillGen: Universal wrapping pipeline converting any medical AI model into MCP-compatible Medical Skills (inspired by TxAgent's ToolGen)
(2) Med-SkillLib: Curated Medical Skills library (wrapping MedSAM, CheXagent, etc. + integrating existing Medical MCP servers)
(3) Med-Agent: LLM-powered agent with active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) for multi-step clinical workflows

The key innovation bridges: (a) general agent skill frameworks lacking medical expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools lacking interoperability.

YOUR OUTPUT:
Generate ONE comprehensive, innovative research idea with the following structure:
a) Challenges — current limitations and key bottlenecks
b) Existing Methods — current approaches and their limitations
c) Motivation — why the problem is important, what gaps to address
d) Proposed Method — detailed technical solution with step-by-step methodology, mathematical formulations, key innovations
e) Technical Details — architecture, algorithms, data flow (include pseudocode for core modules)
f) Expected Outcomes — anticipated improvements, evaluation metrics

Focus on what makes this NOVEL for MICCAI 2026 (medical imaging community). The idea should be technically deep with concrete formulations, not just a system engineering proposal.

(3) Med-Agent: LLM-powered agent with active skill discovery (inspired by MCP-Zero) for multi-step clinical workflows

The key innovation bridges: (a) general agent skill frameworks lacking medical expertise, and (b) isolated medical AI tools lacking interoperability.

content.content:
1→\section{Dataset Construction}
     2→We introduce the three-stage construction process of \ourdata: API collection (\cref{sec:api_collection}), instruction generation (\cref{sec:instruction_generation}), and solution path annotation (\cref{sec:answer_annotation}). All procedures are based on \turbo (\textit{gpt-3.5-turbo-16k}), requiring minimal human supervision and can be easily extended to new APIs. 
     3→
     4→\subsection{API Collection}
     5→\label{sec:api_collection}
     6→We start by introducing RapidAPI and its hierarchy, followed by how we crawl and filter APIs.
     7→
     8→\begin{figure*}[!t]
     9→    \centering
    10→    \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/phase12.pdf}}
    11→    \caption{
    12→    \small{The hierarchy of RapidAPI (left) and the process of instruction generation (right).}
    13→    }
    14→    \label{fig:phase12}
    15→\end{figure*}
    16→
    17→\textbf{RapidAPI Hub} \quad
    18→RapidAPI is a leading API marketplace that connects developers with thousands of real-world APIs, streamlining the process of integrating diverse services into applications. Developers can test and connect with various APIs by registering only a RapidAPI key. All APIs in RapidAPI can be classified into $49$ \textit{coarse-grained} \textbf{categories} (\textcolor{blue}{\href{https://rapidapi.com/categories}{link}}), such as sports, finance, and weather. 
    19→The categories associate an API with the most relevant topic.
    20→Additionally, the hub also provides $500+$ \textit{fine-grained} categorization called \textbf{collections} (\textcolor{blue}{\href{https://rapidapi.com/collections}{link}}), e.g., Chinese APIs and database APIs. APIs in the same collection share a common characteristic and often have similar functionalities or goals.
    21→
    22→\textbf{Hierarchy of RapidAPI} \quad
    23→As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:phase12}, each tool may be composed of multiple APIs. For each tool, we crawl the following information: the name and description of the tool, the URL of the host, and all the available APIs belonging to the tool; for each API, we record its name, description, HTTP method, required parameters, optional parameters, request body, executable code snippets for API call, and an example API call response. This rich and detailed metadata serves as a valuable resource for LLMs to understand and effectively use the APIs, even in a zero-shot manner.
    24→
    25→\textbf{API Filtering} \quad
    26→Initially, we gathered $10,853$ tools ($53,190$ APIs) from RapidAPI. However, the quality and reliability of these APIs can vary significantly. In particular, some APIs may not be well-maintained, such as returning 404 errors or other internal errors.
    27→To this end, we perform a rigorous filtering process (details in \cref{sec:detail_filtering_api}) to ensure that the ultimate tool set of \ourdata is reliable and functional.
    28→Finally, we only retain $3,451$ high-quality tools ($16,464$ APIs).
    29→
    30→\subsection{Instruction Generation}
    31→\label{sec:instruction_generation}
    32→
    33→Different from prior works, we specifically focus on two crucial aspects for instruction generation: (1) \textbf{diversity}: to train LLMs to handle a wide range of API usage scenarios, thereby boosting their generalizability and robustness; and (2) \textbf{multi-tool usage}: to mirror real-world situations that often demand the interplay of multiple tools, improving the practical applicability and flexibility of LLMs. 
    34→To this end, instead of brainstorming instructions from scratch and then searching for relevant APIs, we sample different combinations of APIs and craft various instructions that involve them.
    35→% In practice, this strategy ensures the coverage for all collected APIs.
    36→% Furthermore, we use the RapidAPI hierarchy to identify tool relationships, which aids in the generation of multi-tool instructions.
    37→
    38→\textbf{Generating Instructions for APIs} \quad
    39→Define the total API set as $\sS_\text{API}$, at each time, we sample a few APIs: $\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}} \!=\! \{\text{API}_1, \cdots, \text{API}_\text{N}\}$ from $\sS_\text{API}$. We prompt \turbo to understand the functionalities of these APIs and then generate (1) possible instructions ($\text{Inst}_*$) that involve APIs in $\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}}$, and (2) relevant APIs ($\sS_*^{\text{rel}} \!\subset\!\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}}$) for each instruction ($\text{Inst}_*$), i.e., $\{[\sS_1^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_1], \cdots, [\sS_{\text{N}'}^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_{\text{N}'}]\}$, where $\text{N}'$ denotes the number of generated instances.
    40→These (instruction, relevant API) pairs will be used for training the API retriever in \cref{sec:prelim_exp}.
    41→We use different sampling strategies (introduced later) to cover all APIs and most of their combinations, thus ensuring the diversity of our instructions.
    42→
    43→The prompt for \turbo is composed of (1) a general description of the intended instruction generation task, (2) comprehensive documentation of each API in $\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}}$, which helps \turbo understand their functionality and interplay, and (3) three in-context seed examples $\{\text{seed}_1, \text{seed}_2, \text{seed}_3\}$. Each seed example is an ideal instruction generation written by human experts. These seed examples are leveraged to better regulate \turbo's behavior through in-context learning. In total, we wrote $12$ / $36$ diverse seed examples ($\sS_\text{seed}$) for the single-tool / multi-tool setting, and randomly sampled three examples at each time. Detailed prompts for instruction generation are described in \cref{sec:inst_prompt}. Overall, the generation process can be formulated as follows:
    44→\begin{equation}
    45→    \small
    46→    \begin{aligned}
    47→    \underset{\{\text{API}_1, \cdots, \text{API}_\text{N}\} \in \sS_{\text{API}}, \{\text{seed}_1, \cdots, \text{seed}_3\} \in \sS_\text{seed}}{\text{\turbo}}(\{[\sS_1^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_1], \cdots, [\sS_{\text{N'}}^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_{\text{N}'}]\} | \text{API}_1, \cdots, \text{API}_\text{N}, \text{seed}_1, \cdots, \text{seed}_3).
    48→        \nonumber
    49→    \end{aligned}
    50→    \label{eq:instruction_generation}
    51→\end{equation}
    52→
    53→% \underset{\substack{\{\text{API}_1, \cdots, \text{API}_\text{N}\} \in \sS_{\text{API}}, \\ \{\text{seed}_1, \cdots, \text{seed}_3\} \in \sS_\text{seed}}}{\text{\turbo}}(\{[\sS_1^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_1], \cdots, [\sS_{\text{N'}}^{\text{rel}}, \text{Inst}_{\text{N}'}]\} | \text{API}_1, \cdots, \text{API}_\text{N}, \text{seed}_1, \cdots, \text{seed}_3).
    54→
    55→
    56→\textbf{Sampling Strategies for Different Scenarios} \quad
    57→As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:phase12}, for the \textbf{single-tool instructions (I1)}, we iterate over each tool and generate instructions for its APIs. However, for the \textbf{multi-tool setting}, since the interconnections among different tools in RapidAPI are sparse, random sampling tool combinations from the whole tool set often leads to a series of irrelevant tools that cannot be covered by a single instruction in a natural way. To address the sparsity issue, we leverage the RapidAPI hierarchy information.
    58→Since tools belonging to the same RapidAPI \textit{category} or \textit{collection} are generally related to each other in the functionality and goals, we randomly select $2$-$5$ tools from the same category / collection and sample at most $3$ APIs from each tool to generate the instructions. We denote the generated instructions as \textbf{intra-category multi-tool instructions (I2)} and \textbf{intra-collection multi-tool instructions (I3)}, respectively. Through rigorous human evaluation, we find that instructions generated in this way already have a high diversity that covers various practical scenarios. We also provide visualization for instructions using Atlas (\textcolor{blue}{\href{https://atlas.nomic.ai/map/58aca169-c29a-447a-8f01-0d418fc4d341/030ddad7-5305-461c-ba86-27e1ca79d899}{link}}) to support our claim.
    59→
    60→\begin{figure*}[!t]
    61→    \centering
    62→    \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/answer_anno.pdf}}
    63→    \caption{
    64→    \small{A comparison of our \dfs and conventional CoT or ReACT during model reasoning (left). We show part of the solution path annotation process using \turbo (right).}
    65→    }
    66→    \label{fig:phase3}
    67→\end{figure*}
    68→
    69→After generating the initial set of instructions, we further filter those with the hallucinated relevant APIs by assessing whether they exist in $\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}}$.
    70→Finally, we collect nearly $200$k qualified (instruction, relevant API) pairs, including $87413$, $84815$, and $25251$ instances for I1, I2, and I3, respectively.
    71→
    72→\subsection{Solution Path Annotation}
    73→\label{sec:answer_annotation}
    74→As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:phase3}, given an instruction $\text{Inst}_*$, we prompt \turbo to search for a valid action sequence: $\{a_1, \cdots, a_\text{N}\}$. Such a multi-step decision-making process is cast as a multi-round conversation for \turbo. At each round $t$, the model generates an action $a_t$ based on previous interactions, i.e., $\text{\turbo}(a_t|\{a_1, r_1, \cdots, a_{t-1}, r_{t-1}\}, \text{Inst}_*)$, where $r_*$ denotes the real API response.
    75→For each $a_t$, \turbo should specify its ``thought'',  which API to use, and the specific parameters for this API, i.e., $a_t$ has the following format: ``\texttt{Thought}: $\cdots$, \texttt{API Name}: $\cdots$, \texttt{Parameters}: $\cdots$''.
    76→% Hence the decision space is the Cartesian product of the thought, available APIs, and possible parameters, which is infinite by nature.
    77→
    78→To leverage the \textbf{function call} feature of \turbo, we treat each API as a special function and feed its API documentation into \turbo's function field. In this way, the model understands how to call the API. For each instruction $\text{Inst}_*$, we feed all the sampled APIs $\sS_\text{N}^{\text{sub}}$ to \turbo's as available functions.
    79→To let \turbo finish an action sequence, we define two additional functions, i.e., ``Finish with Final Answer'' and ``Finish by Giving Up''. The former function has a parameter that corresponds to a detailed final answer to the original instruction; while the latter function is designed for cases where the provided APIs cannot complete the original instruction after multiple API call attempts.
    80→% instead of only its relevant APIs $\sS_*^{\text{rel}}$. In this way, the model gains access to a broader scope of APIs and expands the action space.
    81→
    82→\textbf{Depth First Search-based Decision Tree} \quad
    83→In our pilot studies, we find that CoT~\citep{wei2023chainofthought} or ReACT~\citep{yao2022react} has inherent limitations: (1) \textbf{error propagation}: a mistaken action may propagate the errors further and cause the model to be trapped in a faulty loop, such as continually calling an API in a wrong way or hallucinating APIs; (2) \textbf{limited exploration}: CoT or ReACT only explores one possible direction, leading to limited exploration of the whole action space. Hence even GPT-4 often fails to find a valid solution path, making annotation difficult.
    84→
    85→To this end, we propose to construct a decision tree to expand the search space and increase the possibility of finding a valid path. As depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:phase3}, our \dfs allows the model to assess different reasoning paths and choose to either (1) proceed along a promising path or (2) abandon an existing node by calling the ``Finish by Giving Up'' function and expand a new node. During node expansion, to diversify the child nodes and expand the search space, we prompt \turbo with the information of the previously generated nodes and explicitly encourage the model to generate a distinct node. For the searching process, we prefer depth-first search (DFS) instead of breadth-first search (BFS) because the annotation can be finished as long as one valid path is found. Using BFS will cost excessive OpenAI API calls. More details are described in \cref{sec:answer_prompt}.
    86→% before reaching a terminal node (the node of ``Finish with Final Answer'' or ``Finish by Giving Up'').
    87→We perform \dfs for all the generated instructions and only retain those passed solution paths. Ultimately, we generate $126,486$ (instruction, solution path) pairs, which are used to train \ourmodel in \cref{sec:main_exp}. % Although it is possible to construct more training instances, we find that $12,657$ instances already bring satisfying generalization performance.
